rationale: This is a classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction, demonstrating the formation of a stable tert-butyl carbocation from tert-butanol under strong acid (H2SO4) conditions. This carbocation is then trapped by a simple nitrile (acetonitrile) to form a nitrilium ion, which upon hydrolysis yields the corresponding N-tert-butylacetamide. All reactants and products are simple, acyclic, and RDKit-parseable, clearly illustrating the core transformation of an alcohol and a nitrile into an N-substituted amide.
collision_avoidance_note: The reactants are simple and acyclic, avoiding complex ring systems, arenes, or other functional groups that could cause collisions with other named reactions. The use of tert-butanol as the carbocation precursor and acetonitrile as the nitrile is a standard, unambiguous representation of the Ritter reaction, distinct from reactions like the Beckmann rearrangement (oximes to amides) or the Schmidt reaction (azides with carbonyls/alcohols).
